title: Calendula salve for skin
subtitle: A soft golden balm for dry patches and small grazes
excerpt: A soft golden balm made from dried calendula flowers steeped in olive oil, then set with a little beeswax. A kitchen tradition long made for closed minor cuts, grazes, dry patches, and rough skin. About fifteen minutes of work spread over an afternoon, then a small tin that keeps for a year.
sourceNotes:
Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), Botanical.com archive, Calendula officinalis entry. Calendula as a wound-herb in old country medicine and the external infused-oil preparation.

King's American Dispensatory (Felter and Lloyd, 1898), Henriette's Herbal archive. Calendula infused-oil method and the 1:5 herb-to-oil ratio.

EMA HMPC monograph on Calendulae flos (2008), European Medicines Agency. Modern conservative dosing for external preparations.

content.text: Calendula salve is a soft golden balm made from dried calendula flowers steeped in olive oil, then set with a little beeswax. A kitchen tradition long made for closed minor cuts, grazes, dry patches, and rough hands. About fifteen minutes of active work spread over an afternoon; the finished tin keeps a year in a cool cupboard.
Calendula is the classic
vulnerary

of western kitchen medicine, long described as a wound-herb of the old country households. The oil draws the colour and scent out of the dried flowers at low heat over a few hours. For external use on closed skin only.
What you need

items.name: Dried calendula flowers
items.prepNote: whole flowers, not just petals, both work; petals alone steep faster

items.name: Olive oil
items.prepNote: good-quality olive oil, not the cheapest blended kind

items.name: Beeswax pellets
items.prepNote: cosmetic-grade pellets, easier to measure than block beeswax

content.text: The working ratios are 1 part dried herb to 5 parts oil by weight, then 1 part beeswax to 4 parts strained oil. That gives a salve firm at room temperature and soft to the fingertip. A firmer balm uses 1:3 beeswax-to-oil; a softer cream uses 1:6. The 1:4 here is the household standard.
Method
Steep the oil, 2-4 hours at 60-65°C
Set up an
infused oil

. In the top of a double boiler, or a heatproof bowl over a saucepan of barely simmering water, combine {{dried-calendula-flowers}} of dried calendula flowers and {{olive-oil}} of olive oil. Clip a probe thermometer to the side of the bowl with the tip in the oil but not touching the bottom.

Hold the oil at 60-65°C for 2 to 4 hours. Above 70°C the colour and scent break down; below 55°C the oil barely steeps. The probe is the only way to be sure. Stir once an hour. The oil should turn deep gold and smell strongly of calendula by hour 2; hour 4 is the deep end of the steep.
Strain the oil and press the flowers

Take the bowl off the heat. Set a fine sieve over a measuring jug and line it with muslin folded double. Pour the warm oil through the muslin. Gather the corners and squeeze the spent flowers firmly to press out the last of the oil they hold, about five to ten percent of the yield hides there.

You should end up with around 180 ml of deep-gold strained oil; the flowers soak up about ten percent of the original volume. If the oil looks cloudy with bits of plant matter, strain a second time through fresh muslin. Cloudiness shortens the shelf life.
Melt the beeswax into the strained oil

Return the strained oil to the double-boiler bowl. Add {{beeswax-pellets}} of beeswax pellets. Heat gently, 70 to 75°C is plenty, and stir until the wax has melted completely and the mixture is uniformly translucent. About 5 minutes.

Test the set: drip a small spoon of the warm mixture onto a cold saucer and let it sit for 30 seconds. The drop should feel firm to a fingertip touch but soft and yielding, not waxy and hard. If it is too soft, melt in another 5 g of beeswax; if it is too firm, add 10 ml more strained oil. Adjust now while the mixture is still in the bowl.
Pour into the tin and set

Off the heat. Pour the warm mixture through a small funnel into a clean salve tin (or a sterilised jam jar). Leave the lid off for the first 10 minutes so steam can escape; a sealed lid traps condensation and shortens the shelf life. Once the surface has dulled from glossy to matte, lid the tin and label it with the date and the herb.
How to use

Apply a thin layer (a pea-sized dab covers the back of a hand) to clean, closed skin two or three times a day. The thin layer is the rule: a thick coat seals the skin under a film of beeswax. For dry patches, expect to use the salve daily for a week before judging whether it suits the skin. Use on minor closed grazes once the skin has scabbed, not on bleeding or broken skin.
Calendula is in the daisy family. If you have reacted to chamomile, ragweed, yarrow, or chrysanthemum before,
patch-test
first: dab a small amount on the inside of the forearm and wait a day before wider use.
Storage

The salve keeps for a year in a sealed tin in a cool, dark cupboard. If the surface develops white spots, or the salve smells sharp and paint-like rather than herbal, discard the tin and start a new batch; the oil has gone off. A tin left somewhere hot melts and resets with an oily layer on top, which is harmless but unsightly.

attrs.intro: The four ways a calendula salve goes wrong, and what to fix.

items.fix: Re-melt the salve, stir in 5 to 10 g more beeswax pellets, test the set on a cold saucer before re-pouring.
items.cause: Not enough beeswax for the volume of oil, or the beeswax did not fully melt.
items.symptom: Salve too soft, doesn't hold its shape in the tin

items.fix: Re-melt, add 20 ml more strained infused oil (or plain olive oil if you have run out of infused), test the set, re-pour.
items.cause: Too much beeswax for the volume of oil.
items.symptom: Salve too hard, won't pick up on a fingertip

items.fix: Source fresh dried calendula from a herbal supplier; the smell from the bag should be strong, not faint. Re-do the steep with the probe thermometer holding the oil at 60-65°C.
items.cause: Oil ran too cool (under 55°C) during the steep, or the dried flowers were stale.
items.symptom: Salve smells thin and oily, not herbal

items.fix: Discard the tin. Next batch: make sure the flowers are fully dry, leave the lid off the warm-pour for 10 minutes before sealing, store somewhere drier.
items.cause: Water made it into the tin, from un-dried flowers, condensation under a too-soon-sealed lid, or a damp bathroom.
items.symptom: White spots on the surface after a few weeks

attrs.heading: If the salve went sideways

attrs.title: A note
attrs.body: Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional for ongoing or serious symptoms.
